definition_short: Random Access Memory is a type of computer memory that can be accessed randomly; any byte of memory can be accessed without touching the preceding bytes.
definition_detailed: Random Access Memory (RAM) is the main memory in a computer. It is much faster to read from and write to than other kinds of storage, such as a hard disk drive (HDD), solid-state drive (SSD) or optical drive. Data is stored in RAM temporarily while the computer is running, and is lost when the power is turned off (volatile memory). RAM allows the computer to work with more information at the same time, which usually has a considerable effect on total system performance.
analogy: Think of RAM like your desk surface. It's where you put everything you're working on right now so you can reach it quickly. Your hard drive is like a filing cabinet in the corner—it holds much more, but it takes longer to get things out of it.
examples: Loading a game's assets into memory for fast access during gameplay.
Keeping an open document available for immediate editing.

definition_short: Read-Only Memory is a type of non-volatile memory used in computers and other electronic devices.
definition_detailed: Read-Only Memory (ROM) is a type of storage medium that permanently stores data on computers and other electronic devices. It contains the programming needed to start a computer, which is essential for boot-up; it performs major input/output tasks and holds programs or software instructions. Because ROM is read-only, it cannot be changed; it is permanent and non-volatile, meaning it also holds its memory even when power is removed.
analogy: Think of ROM like a printed book. You can read the information in it as many times as you want, but you can't easily change what's written on the pages. It's always there, even when you close the book and put it away.
examples: The BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) on a computer motherboard.
Firmware in a microwave or a car's engine control unit.

definition_short: The collection of functional units that perform data processing operations in a CPU.
definition_detailed: In computer architecture, a datapath is the collection of functional units (such as ALUs, registers, and buses) that perform data processing operations. It is the 'muscle' of the CPU, while the control unit is the 'brain' that tells the datapath what to do. The datapath is responsible for the actual execution of instructions, including arithmetic, logical, and data movement operations.
analogy: Think of the datapath like the kitchen in a restaurant. It has all the tools (ALU, registers) to cook the food (process data). The control unit is like the chef, who gives the orders on what to cook and when.
examples: The ALU performing an addition operation.
Registers storing intermediate results of a calculation.
Buses moving data between the ALU and memory.

definition_short: The Central Processing Unit is the primary component of a computer that acts as its 'control center.'
definition_detailed: The Central Processing Unit (CPU) is the electronic circuitry within a computer that executes instructions that make up a computer program. The CPU performs basic arithmetic, logic, controlling, and input/output (I/O) operations specified by the instructions. Most modern CPUs are microprocessors, meaning they are contained on a single integrated circuit (IC) chip. A CPU typically contains a control unit, an arithmetic logic unit (ALU), and various registers.
analogy: The CPU is like the brain of the computer. It receives instructions, processes them, and tells all the other parts of the computer what to do.
examples: Calculating the result of a mathematical formula in a spreadsheet.
Processing the logic of a character's movement in a video game.
Executing the code that renders a webpage in your browser.
